transcript.presenting_concern: Processing childhood trauma — car accident loss of sibling

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Angela, how are you feeling coming in today? I know last session's preparation work brought up a lot. Take your time.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I've been thinking about it all week. The image we identified — the car, the rain, my brother Danny — it's been coming up more frequently. In dreams, during quiet moments. Not exactly nightmares... more like the scene replaying on a loop. Sometimes just a flash — his face — and then it's gone.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's not uncommon after target identification. When we bring a traumatic memory into focus and prepare it for processing, the brain sometimes begins working on it between sessions — like it's warming up. How distressing have the dreams been, on our 0-10 scale?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Maybe a 5 or 6. They're different from what I used to get. The old nightmares — the flashbacks — those were like reliving it. Heart pounding, drenched in sweat, genuinely confused about where I was when I woke up. These are more like... sad watching. Like I'm viewing a movie of that night. Still emotional, but I'm not IN it the same way.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That distinction is really important and I want to highlight it. Your nervous system may already be beginning to shift from a trauma response — the reliving quality, the hyperarousal, the confusion between past and present — to a grief response: sadness, loss, missing. Reliving says "this is happening NOW." Grieving says "this happened THEN, and it's unbearably sad." The second is painful, but it's not traumatic in the same way. It means your brain is starting to place this in the past where it belongs.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I hadn't thought about it that way. It IS different. Sad versus terrifying.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Yes. Today we'll begin active processing — bilateral stimulation with the target memory. Let me remind you of the procedure. You'll bring up the target image — the worst moment — along with the negative cognition we identified and the body sensations associated with it. I'll provide bilateral stimulation using eye movements — you'll follow my fingers. We'll pause periodically and you'll tell me whatever comes up — thoughts, images, emotions, body sensations. There's no right or wrong response. Whatever your brain produces is what we work with. You're always in control. Say "stop" at any time if you need a break. If it becomes too intense, we'll use the container or safe place exercises we practiced. How are you feeling about starting?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Ready. Nervous, but ready. I want to do this. I've been carrying this for twenty-three years. I'm tired.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That fatigue of carrying it — let's use that motivation. Before we begin, let me do a quick body check. Right now, sitting here, what's your current distress level, 0-10?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: About a 3. Just the anticipatory anxiety of knowing what we're about to do.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay. And your safe place — the garden — can you access it quickly if you need to?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yes. Sun, warm soil, planting something. I can get there fast.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Alright. Let's begin. Bring up the target image — the worst moment from the accident. Let it form clearly in your mind.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [closes eyes, then opens them] The backseat. I'm twelve. It's November — cold, dark, rainy. We're driving home from my cousin's birthday party. Danny is in the front passenger seat. He's fourteen. Mom is driving. The rain is so heavy — the wipers are going as fast as they can but you still can't see well. The road is a two-lane highway. And then... headlights. Coming at us from the wrong side of the road. Too fast. Way too fast. Danny turned around to look at me — he was smiling because we'd been joking about our cousin's terrible karaoke — and that's the last image. His face, smiling, turning back to look at me. And then the impact.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Hold that image — Danny's face turning back, the smile, and then the impact. The negative cognition — the belief about yourself connected to this?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: "I should have died instead."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: How true does "I should have died instead" feel right now, 1 to 7, where 1 is completely false and 7 is completely true?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Six. It feels almost like a fact. Like the universe made a mistake taking him instead of me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Where do you feel this in your body right now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Stomach. A heavy stone, right here [touches abdomen]. Like something I swallowed that won't move. And my throat — tight. Constricted. Like I want to scream but can't get it out. It's been stuck there for twenty-three years.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And your distress level — the SUD — 0 to 10?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Eight.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay. Hold the image — Danny's face turning back, the smile. Hold the belief — "I should have died instead." Notice the stone in your stomach and the tightness in your throat. Keep all of that present and follow my fingers. [BLS set 1 — approximately 30 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let it go. Take a breath. Blank it out for a moment. [pause] What do you notice?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The sound. I forgot the sound — or I'd blocked it. The screeching of tires. The sound of metal on metal — it's this horrific crunching sound. And then my mother screaming. She screamed his name. "Danny! Danny!" Over and over and over. I couldn't move — I was pinned by something, the seat in front of me had come back. I could hear her screaming but I couldn't see either of them. Everything was dark and sideways and wrong.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Stay with that — the sounds, the confusion, being pinned. Go with that. [BLS set 2 — approximately 30 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Take a breath. What comes up now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [crying] The guilt. Mom was hurt too — broken collarbone, cut on her forehead, she hit her head on the steering wheel. But she was reaching for Danny, not for me. Reaching and screaming and reaching. She didn't check on me. She didn't say my name. She didn't say "Angela, are you okay?" And I KNOW — I know rationally — she was in shock. Danny was the most injured, he was unconscious, there was blood. Of course she went to him first. Any mother would. But twelve-year-old me... twelve-year-old me heard silence where her name should have been. And thought: "she doesn't care if I'm okay because she wishes it had been me instead of him."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That twelve-year-old's interpretation — "she wishes it was me." Stay with that child's experience. Notice where that belief lives in your body right now. [BLS set 3 — approximately 30 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Breathe. What's present?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: The loneliness. After the accident — in the hospital — everyone was focused on Danny. He was in surgery, then ICU. My mom was in her own hospital bed. I had a broken wrist and bruised ribs but I was "fine" by comparison. My aunt came to be with me but all she talked about was Danny. "Danny's in surgery, pray for Danny." And I was lying there with a broken wrist thinking "I'M here too. I'm hurt too. Does anyone see me?" And then... when Danny died the next morning... I felt like I wasn't allowed to be in pain anymore. My pain didn't matter because his was bigger. He was dead and I was alive and alive meant I had no right to suffer.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: "No right to suffer." That's profound. The invisible child — present but unseen, alive but unentitled to pain. Go with that. [BLS set 4 — approximately 30 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What's here?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Something's shifting. I'm seeing the scene from outside now — like a camera pulled back and I'm watching from above. I can see the car — it's crushed on the passenger side. I can see my mom reaching for Danny. But I also see... a paramedic. She's on my side of the car. She's opening my door. She's a woman with dark hair in a uniform and she's saying "I've got you, sweetheart. You're going to be okay. I'm right here." She's holding my hand and she's looking right at me. I forgot about her completely. For twenty-three years I forgot that someone came for me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Someone came for you. You weren't invisible to everyone. Let yourself feel that — being seen, being held, being told you'd be okay. Notice where that lands in your body. [BLS set 5 — approximately 30 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What do you notice?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [still crying but breathing more evenly] The stone in my stomach is lighter. Not gone, but... smaller. And my throat — the scream — it feels less stuck. Like some of it came out just now with the crying. And I'm feeling something I haven't felt about this memory before: warmth. That paramedic — she was warm. Her hand was warm. She cared about me specifically. She said MY name — she must have asked or found out somehow — she said "Angela, you're safe."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Stay with that warmth, that specific care directed at you. "Angela, you're safe." [BLS set 6 — approximately 25 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What's coming up?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [smiling through tears] Danny. Not the accident Danny — alive Danny. I'm seeing him at his fourteenth birthday party, two weeks before the accident. He blew out the candles but blew too hard and cake frosting went everywhere — on his face, on the table, a blob hit me on the cheek. And we laughed until we cried. Mom was laughing too. We were all laughing so hard. That's a real memory. And I haven't been able to access it in YEARS. Every time I try to think about Danny, the accident image takes over. But right now I can see him laughing with frosting on his nose.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The trauma memory was overwriting the relational memories — the loving ones. It was so loud and so dominant that it blocked everything else about Danny. As we process the trauma and reduce its intensity, the other memories — the beautiful ones — become accessible again. He's more than the worst moment. Hold that birthday image — the laughter, the frosting, the joy. [BLS set 7 — approximately 25 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What's here now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: More memories. Coming like... like water that's been dammed up finally breaking through. Danny teaching me to ride a bike. Danny defending me from a bully in the park — he was two years older and he stepped right in front of this kid and said "that's my sister, back off." Danny and I watching scary movies with a blanket over our heads and screaming at the same time. [laughing and crying simultaneously] He used to make these TERRIBLE puns. He called me "Angel-a" because he said I was his angel sent to annoy him. I haven't thought of any of this in years. It's all been locked behind the accident.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You're getting him back, Angela. Not just the trauma — him. The actual person he was. The relationship you had. That's the gift of this processing — you don't lose the grief, but you gain back the love that was trapped behind it.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [deep breath] That's exactly what it feels like. Like I've had this person locked away and I can finally visit him again. The real him. Not just the dying him.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let's do one more set with the positive memories present. Hold whatever feels most alive right now. [BLS set 8 — approximately 25 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Take a breath. How are you doing?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Tired. Emotional. But... lighter? That's the word that keeps coming. Lighter. Like I set down a bag of bricks I've been carrying so long I forgot how heavy it was.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Let me check in now. Bring up the original target image — the car, the rain, Danny's face turning back, the smile, and then the impact. Let it form. What's the SUD — the distress level — now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause, concentrating] A four. Maybe... maybe a three. It's sad. Deeply, deeply sad. It's the saddest thing in my life and it always will be. But it doesn't feel like it's happening to me right now. The terror is gone. The reliving quality is gone. It feels like... the past. A terrible thing that happened in the past to a twelve-year-old girl, and that girl survived.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: From an eight to a three-to-four in one processing session. That's significant movement. Now — the belief "I should have died instead." How true does that feel right now, 1 to 7?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [long pause] Two. Maybe even a 1.5 if that's allowed. Because... Danny wouldn't have wanted that. He loved me. He was smiling at me in his last moment because he loved me. He'd never want me dead in his place. He was the kind of big brother who protected his little sister — he wouldn't want protection at the cost of her life. And my mom — I know now that she didn't wish it was me. She was in shock. She was reaching for the one who was most hurt. That's not the same as not caring about me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: You're making meaning — differentiated, adult meaning — of a child's terrified interpretation. The twelve-year-old believed "she wishes it was me." The thirty-five-year-old understands "she was in shock and responded to the most critical situation." Both can be true at the same time — the child's feeling was real, AND the adult's understanding is more accurate.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yes. I can hold both. I can be compassionate toward that scared twelve-year-old without believing her interpretation is the whole truth.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Beautiful. Now — the positive cognition we identified in preparation: "I deserve to be here, and I honor Danny by living fully." How true does that feel, 1 to 7?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [pause] A five. It's getting there. It's not automatic yet — like, I can't say it without a small voice protesting. But I can feel it becoming possible. A week ago it was maybe a two. Something opened today.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: A five is strong progress for a first processing session. Let's install that a bit. Hold the positive belief — "I deserve to be here, and I honor Danny by living fully" — and notice how it feels in your body. Where does that truth live? [BLS set 9 — brief installation, approximately 20 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: What do you notice?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Warmth in my chest. The opposite of the stone in my stomach. Like a spreading warmth. And the throat — it's more open. Like the scream that was stuck there — some of it released today.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Let's strengthen that. Hold the warmth, the openness, the belief. [BLS set 10 — brief installation, approximately 20 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: How's that landing?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Solid. More solid than I expected. I can feel a difference in my body — like I'm sitting differently. My shoulders are lower. I didn't realize how clenched I've been.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Trauma lives in the body. As it releases, the body changes its posture — literally. The armoring softens. Now, let me do a body scan with you. Close your eyes. Start at the top of your head and scan down. Tell me if you notice any residual disturbance — tension, pain, heaviness, anything uncomfortable.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [scanning] Head is fine. Face is fine — tear-streaked but fine. [small laugh] Throat — still a little tight but much better than before. Shoulders — down and relaxed. Chest — warm. Stomach — the stone is still there but it's small now. Like a pebble instead of a boulder. Hips, legs — okay. Actually my legs feel heavy. Like I ran a marathon. Tired heavy.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That heaviness in the legs is a normal processing response — emotional work is exhausting, and the body needs to discharge the activation. The pebble in the stomach — we'll continue working with that in future sessions. It may represent residual grief that needs more processing, or it may resolve between sessions as your brain continues integrating what we did today. Let's close with your safe place. Can you go to the garden now? Let it form.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [closes eyes] I'm in the garden. It's afternoon — warm sun. I can feel the soil in my hands — I'm planting something. Lavender, I think. It smells good. It's quiet and peaceful. There's a breeze. I'm alone but not lonely — it's chosen solitude. Peaceful solitude.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Feel the warmth, the soil, the lavender scent. You're safe here. Nothing is required of you. [BLS — brief safe place installation, approximately 15 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And now I want to add something to the garden, if you're open to it. Is there a place in the garden where Danny could be remembered? Not the accident Danny — the laughing, frosting-on-his-nose, terrible-pun-telling Danny?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [still eyes closed, smiling] There's a bench. Under a tree. And his name is carved in it. And when I sit there, I can remember him — the real him — without it hurting the same way. I can visit him there and feel love instead of guilt.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Hold that image — the bench, his name, the love. [BLS — brief installation, approximately 15 seconds]

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Take a deep breath. Open your eyes when you're ready.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [opens eyes, exhales deeply] Okay. I'm here.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: How are you feeling right now?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Tired. Deeply tired. But lighter. And... grateful, maybe? Like something enormous happened and I'm still standing. Also a little raw — like a scab that got pulled off. Tender underneath.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's an apt description. You ARE raw. Processing opens up areas that have been sealed for decades, and they need time to heal in a new way — not re-sealed, but genuinely healed. Between now and our next session, you may experience continued processing. Dreams may shift or intensify. Emotions may surface unexpectedly — tears during a song, a sudden wave of sadness or relief in the grocery store. Memories of Danny — the good ones — may keep appearing. All of this is normal and it's your brain continuing the work we started today.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: What do I do if it gets too intense?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: First, use the safe place — the garden. Go there mentally, hold it, breathe until you settle. Second, the container exercise — if overwhelming material surfaces, imagine putting it in the container and closing the lid. It's not gone forever; it just means "not right now, I'll bring this to session." Third, ground yourself — five things you see, four you hear, three you touch. And fourth, journal. Write whatever comes up. Don't censor it. Bring the journal next week and we'll incorporate anything important.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I've been journaling already — last week, after our preparation session, I wrote six pages.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Keep going. The writing helps integrate — it moves experience from the emotional brain into language and narrative, which is part of how trauma resolves. One more thing, Angela — I want to check: the belief "I should have died instead" — when you leave here today, if that thought arises during the week, what can you tell yourself?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: That Danny wouldn't want that. That I was a child and it wasn't my choice. That my mom was in shock, not choosing between us. And that... I deserve to be here. I honor him by living fully. It's not all the way automatic yet, but I can reach for it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Reaching for it is enough right now. It'll become more automatic with more processing. We'll continue this work next session — checking in on where the SUD is, whether the positive cognition has strengthened further, and addressing any new material that emerges between now and then.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Will we do more bilateral stimulation?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Yes. We may need to do another round on this same target if the SUD hasn't continued to decrease, or we may move to related targets — for example, the hospital experience you described, the feeling of being invisible, or your relationship with your mother in the aftermath. Complex grief and childhood trauma often have multiple connected memory nodes, and we process them systematically.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: My relationship with my mom... yeah. There's a lot there. After Danny died, she kind of... folded in on herself. For years. I lost my brother and then I lost my mother to grief. She was there physically but she was gone. I became the caretaker — at twelve, thirteen, fourteen. Making sure she ate, reminding her to pay bills. I grew up overnight.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's important material, and we'll get there. Today was enormous — you don't need to carry all of it right now. For this week, be gentle with yourself. Rest. Let the processing happen without forcing it. And notice — really notice — if more of Danny's good memories surface. Let yourself have them. They're yours.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I want them. I want to remember him as he was, not just how he died.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's the goal. And today, you got a taste of it. The frosting. The puns. "Angel-a." He's still accessible to you — he's been waiting behind the trauma for you to come find him again.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [tears falling quietly] Thank you. I think I needed to hear that.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Angela, what you did today took immense courage. You faced the worst moment of your life — a moment you've been fleeing from for twenty-three years — and you moved through it. You didn't fragment, you didn't shut down, you didn't leave the room. You stayed present with the pain and let it transform. That's not weakness. That's the opposite of weakness.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I don't feel weak. For the first time in a long time, I feel... strong? Like maybe I'm someone who can handle hard things. Like maybe surviving wasn't the terrible thing I always thought it was.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It wasn't a terrible thing. It was a gift. A painful, complicated, unasked-for gift. And you're learning how to unwrap it.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Same time next week?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Same time. Rest tonight. Be kind to yourself. The journal, the garden, Danny's bench — they're all there for you. Call my office if anything feels like an emergency between now and then.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I will. Thank you, Dr. Morrison. Today was... I don't have words for it yet. But it was important.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It was. And you were brave. See you next week, Angela.

goals: Processing childhood trauma — car accident loss of sibling
